issue_title: Parameters in command line are not parsed and not painted
issue_body:
What I expect to see (picture from tldr main page)
![tldr-expectations](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/946520/12147291/f82785fa-b4b9-11e5-9fb3-2c57d484215d.png)

What I actually see
![bwh1te bwh1te-peka _005](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/946520/12147168/604f729c-b4b9-11e5-857a-f178a20d6567.png)

As we can see on this screenshot, param pattern `{{user_param}}` is not parsing by python client. Since it doesn't parse, it can't be painted like it does on first pic.

What we should do:
1. Add new `DEFAULT_COLORS` entry (`parameter` for example)
2. Parse `command` lines for `{{param}}` patterns and paint in `parameter` color
3. Another parts of the line should be painted in `command` color
4. Except the first two spaces (as we can see on first pic)

issue_title: Add "last modified" attribute to the gradebook models
issue_body: In certain cases, it is useful to know when the last time something was modified in the gradebook. For example, in order to determine whether feedback should be regenerated (see #926) you need to be able to know when the last time grading took place, and compare that timestamp to the timestamp of the feedback.

issue_title: split cell at cursor into new notebook & ipynb file concatenation - feature requests
issue_body:
The split cell at cursor functionality is nice. Is there a way to split it into a new notebook?  Also, is there is a command line tool to concatenate or even split(at a particular line number) two notebooks? It would useful feature in the data scientist work flow.

For the second request, a quick search turned this up which looks nice.
https://gist.github.com/fperez/e2bbc0a208e82e450f69

issue_title: Clustering multiple GPUs from different machines and remotely run Jupyter Notebook
issue_body:
Hello everyone, I am a newcomer here in this community. 
I have only 2 GB GPU but my friend has 4GB so I generally train my model on his machine. I normally use Jupyter Notebook and I code in Python. Recently I came to know about "[Running a notebook server](http://jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.io/en/latest/public_server.html)" and I set up that. Now I can remotely run a jupyter notebook on my machine (client) while the resources are used from my friend's machine (server).
4 GB of GPU is also not sufficient for me. I am curious if I could remotely use GPUs from many of my friends' machine and cluster them and then remotely run the jupyter notebook. Its similar to the server-client model that we previously created but I wish to extend it to multiple "shared-servers" so that I can use all of their GPU's in collaborative and distributive fashion. It is a kind of 'many-to-one' server (many) and client (one) model.

Can anybody help me how can I achieve that in Jupyter Notebook server ? Or is there any option to remotely use GPU from different machines and run my python code remotely ?
**It will be very kind if you could forward me to appropriate place where I could find a solution**

Thanks
The menu bar is using valuable screen space when displaying notebooks (With large font size, so that the content is visible).

issue_title: Download as HTML puts "+" symbols instead of spaces in filename
issue_body:
Using the File > Download > As HTML... command, I would expect the downloaded HTML file to have the same filename as the Jupyter Notebook.

However, every space is replaced by a + symbol.

Versions:
Python 3.6.1
IPython 5.3.0
Jupyter 1.0.0
Windows 10 Pro x64

issue_title: Long running jobs in the cloud
issue_body:
In terms of reproducible research, this is just spectacular! Often times, some next generation sequencing experiments (life sciences) will require large compute machines, resources, etc. so cloud computing is ideal. Incorporating the ability to leverage these large machines through notebooks is possible for short jobs, but not long jobs (>1 hr).

Is it possible to perform analysis as described above but "detach" from the machine instead of having to stay logged in the entire time (e.g. Amazon EC2)?

issue_title: Create a command line tool for troubleshooting installation
issue_body:
@takluyver @minrk @carreau If we don't already have it, it may be helpful to create a command line tool that the end user could run to check their installation and report the most important information that is typically needed to assist them. 

In addition or alternatively, a notebook that would display to the user all of the parameters that they currently have configured might be useful for them too as something that could visually display the settings. We have some of this in the Help menu but having it as a notebook might be helpful for the user in self debugging.

issue_title: Non-editable cells can have their cell type changed
issue_body: It seems that even when cells have the `"editable": false` in their metadata, their cell type can still be changed (even though the source cannot). This seems counterintuitive to me and causes problems with nbgrader as well (see https://github.com/jupyter/nbgrader/issues/878 ). I will make a PR shortly to address the issue.

issue_title: Ensure list of keyboard shortcuts is complete
issue_body:
I happened to press **⌘d** (cmd-d) in edit mode, and it deleted the selected text. ⌘d deletes the whole line when no text is selected.
This is not mentioned anywhere in the list of keyboard shortcuts.

This, of course, leads me to wonder what other shortcuts might be available that are not listed.

Incidentally I used that shortcut to attempt Sublime-style multi-cursor select. I saw mention of this feature [on twitter](https://twitter.com/projectjupyter/status/572996330353639424), but there is no mention in the keyboard shortcut pane or the docs of how to activate it.

Running Jupyter Notebook 4.0.6

issue_title: Jupyter notebook slow to render computational results even with very short computational timings
issue_body:
My notebooks are very slow to output the results of code. Using the ExecuteTime plugin, I can see that some code takes 1 - 2 ms to complete, but the cell output doesn't render for 60s or more. 

This can be extremely trivial code, but the results are the same. The "running" asterix will show in the prompt for many magnitudes longer than the actual computation takes. 

I have a number of plugins install, they all come from the nbextensions package and additionally the VIM bindings plugin. I have tested this on the Brave browser and Firefox. I'm running Ubuntu Gnome 16.04.

Maybe related 1426, but I can kind of reproduce the slowdown in updating streams in the browser UI:
```
import sys
s=800
L=80

for j in range(30):
    print('')
    for i in range(s):
        p = int(L*i/s)+1
        print('\r' +str(j)+'='*p+'-'*(L-p), end='')
        sys.stdout.flush()
```

You can see the the first line is updated smoothly, and the more new line are added, the more rough/jerky, the updates are. 

My **guess** is that find the `\n` in the string becomes a limiting factor, I've also read some things about string concatenation (or split) in JS having a bad n^2 complexity in some case. 

If that's the case, we might be able to store the output of N lines efficiently:  `[Line 1 to N-1 , Line N]`, which makes concatenation, or erasing the last line, both efficient operations.

issue_title: Should notebooks allow editing when saving is forbidden?
issue_body:
Sometimes the notebook gets a small "forbidden" notification in yellow in the top right status bar, and it is impossible to save work. I gather that to make the "forbidden" notification go away it is necessary to refresh the page and then enter a password/token.

However, it is still possible to edit the notebook and execute code in this state. Because the notification is not so salient or informative, there have now been several instances where I have done quite a bit of work in a notebook that cannot be saved, which is ultimately (and unfortunately) lost.

I have not really invested the time to learn the inner workings of the notebook security model, so my understanding is quite naive. But I think either the salience of the notification should be enhanced (and an explanation of its consequences should be readily available), or the notebook should simply not allow editing/execution if it's not going to be possible to save the work.

issue_title: Multiple env.sh files from planemo dependency_script?
issue_body:
The initial ``planemo dependency_script`` implementation f798c7e (see #303 and #310) produces a single ``dep_install.sh`` and ``env.sh`` script even when called on multiple ``tool_dependencies.xml`` files. The simplistic usage model is to run ``source env.sh`` prior to running Galaxy or any Galaxy tests.

In general do we need a way to produce separate ``env.sh`` scripts and put them where Galaxy will use automatically them prior to tool execution? [I don't know how that works currently...]

--

The simple model can work fine, e.g. peterjc/galaxy_blast@a1303d7 and https://travis-ci.org/peterjc/galaxy_blast/builds/84294442 uses ``planemo dependency_script`` to run two ``tool_dependencies.xml`` files to install BLAST+ 2.2.31 and Blast2GO.

However, if I wanted to also install BLAST+ 2.2.30 there would be a clash of binary names. There is a similar problem with https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_mira where I want to  install three versions of the MIRA assembly suite, which all have a binary ``mira`` and thus cannot simply be put in the same ``$INSTALL_DIR``. Calling ``planemo dependency_script`` three times with different ``$INSTALL_DIR`` paths works.

However, if ``$PATH`` was used to find the ``mira`` binary this still present a problem as there are competing answers - likewise with different versions of BLAST+ on ``$PATH``.

For MIRA, I am already use ``$MIRA3``, ``$MIRA4`` and ``$MIRA4_9`` to hopefully avoid this...

issue_title: Custom styles should be able to use the application-package-names setting
issue_body:
Currently, `checker.py` uses the `application-package-names` option only if the name of the style matches a whitelist: `['appnexus', 'edited']`:
https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8-import-order/blob/35434d29ddc9c1a580ae0c9ff14b8f00a288fd99/flake8_import_order/checker.py#L44

That means that it is not possible to create a custom style that extends the behaviour of the `AppNexus` or `Edited` styles (ie. that uses `application-package-names` option) unless that style also uses the same name.

I would suggest that instead of comparing the name, a property be added the base `Style` class like `accepts_application_package_names` which is set to `False` in the `Style` class but set to `True` in the `AppNexus` and `Edited` classes. Then the logic in `checker.py` should be changed to check for this flag in the style class.

Consider this watcher_getter:

```
 proc = watcher_getter(
        name=service_path('dragoman/bin/dragoman'),
        kwargs=dict(
            env=dict(
                PORT=str(port),
                STORAGE_URI=dragoman_db_uri,
            ),
        ),
        checker=functools.partial(airportlocker_listening, port),
    )
```

Causes this TypeError:

```
name = '/home/vagrant/.virtualenvs/gryphon/services/dragoman/bin/dragoman', arguments = None
kwargs = {'env': {'PORT': '9000', 'STORAGE_URI': "mongodb://['localhost:47667']/translations"}}
timeout = 20, checker = <functools.partial object at 0x7fef84e1e730>

    def watcher_getter_function(name, arguments=None, kwargs=None, timeout=20, checker=None):
        executable = find_executable(name)
        assert executable, 'You have to install {0} executable.'.format(name)
    
>       cmd = [name] + arguments or []
E       TypeError: can only concatenate list (not "NoneType") to list

/home/vagrant/.virtualenvs/gryphon/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pytest_services/service.py:63: TypeError
```

The issue is with the bind order of that expression. A distilled example is this:

```
>>> [] + None or []
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: can only concatenate list (not "NoneType") to list
```

Probably what was intended was this:

```
>>> [] + (None or [])
[]
```

One can workaround the problem by supplying `arguments=[]`, but it would be better if services handled its own defaults.

issue_title: How to handle assemblies that fail to resolve now there is no DomainContext.AssemblyResolve event?

issue_body:
This is a continuation of my previous bug report that was placed poorly ( see: dotnet/corefx/issues/31803 ). In general this is a request about getting the old JScript .NET updated to work with more modern ECMAScript Syntax and on other platforms. In general the base ECMAScript standard has adopted some features found in C# that can be used to help with implementing a updated version of the language.

Major Implementation Problems ( Specification wise):

* The following keywords are not supported yet: interface, package, private protected, and public. These are highly useful for data management in .net. However we can use the JScript.NET syntax for now on this.
* Syntax for specifying Data types ahead of time is not set yet, but a suggestion on this has been made ( see: https://ecmascript-daily.github.io/pages/status-of-static-typing-in-ecmascript/ ). This effects the following...
  * Variable data types
  * function parameter data types.
  * function return data type.
* Classes can only have 1 super class - This is solved by using class inheritance from expressions. 

Elements helping with Implementation: 
* ECMAScript 2015 (aka ECMAScript 6th edition) ( see: https://github.com/rse/es6-features or http://es6-features.org/ for examples) - This version of ECMAScript adds most of the necessary elements for .NET language Bindings. In general the major features that can be used are as follows...
  * Block Scoping for variables and functions 
  * Lamda Opeator ( aka Arrow Functions) - This feature is symantically similar to the C# Lambda expressions.
  * Lexical This for classes and arrow functions.
  * Unicode Strings and regular expressions.
  * Constant variables
  * Default values for function paramaters.
  * Template Literals - This could pose a few problems, but in general this is extremely useful for string interpolation.
  * Generator functions - These behave a lot like the C# generator functions, but the syntax is different.
  * import/export statements ( aka Modules) - The import statements from JScript.NET do not need any changes here, but there is still some major differences that need addressing.
  * Classes - Some basic Syntax changes from JScript.NET did are required, but in general the format is a lot cleaner. The only issue is as follows... 
     * class constructors use the function "constructor" now.
     * methods no longer require function keyword in classes.
     * static member functions.
  * strings support new search methods ( "startsWith", "endsWith", and "includes".
  * Proxy handlers.
  * Typed Arrays - This is useful for static types like signed integers, unsigned integers, single precision floating point, and double precision floating point.
* ECMAScript 2016 ( aka ECMAScript 7th edition) - This mostly cleans up implementation details for ECMAScript 2015. The new features for ECMAScript 2017 are the exponentiation operator ( ** ) and Array.prototype.includes.
* ECMAScript 2017 - This adds a lot more features.
  * Shared Memory and Atomics ( see: https://github.com/tc39/ecmascript_sharedmem )
  * async Functions parameters( see: https://tc39.github.io/ecmascript-asyncawait/  )
  * String Padding ( see: https://github.com/tc39/proposal-string-pad-start-end )

Side References which can help with implementing the Feature:
* Jscript .NET Reference Pages (From Visual Studio 2010 ) - https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/visualstudio/visual-studio-2010/x85xxsf4(v%3dvs.100)
* TypeScript ( https://www.typescriptlang.org/ ) - The syntax for typed functions and variables is a clear match to what is used in JScript.NET.
* Scala.js ( https://www.scala-js.org/ ) - This also follows some of the symantics used by JScript .NET.
* JS++ by Onux Corp ( see: https://www.onux.com/jspp/ ) - This follows a more c-like approach to defining variable types.

issue_title: Apache plugin's cleanup() doesn't remove SSL vhosts created during auth
issue_body: If you run `letsencrypt auth` for an Apache virtual host that doesn't have SSL enabled, the Apache plugin creates a new vhost `*-le-ssl.conf`. After performing the challenges, the plugin's `cleanup` function is called but it doesn't remove the created vhosts. Instead, the user has to run `letsencrypt` again and the files will be deleted up when `recovery_routine` is called. These temporary files should be removed during the `cleanup` function.

issue_title: LetsEncrypt's Apache plugin uses dummy placeholders in the generated apache conf
issue_body:
When using letsencrypt-auto, the generated apache vhost file is created correctly but the location of the certs is not specified in the file : 

i.e.
```

It looks like this is quite intentional in the code but im not sure why (https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L579)

SLCertificateFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/cert.pem
SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/privkey.pem
SSLCertificateChainFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/fullchain.pem

issue_title: After calling close, consumers issue periodic metadata requests
issue_body:
If you create a consumer, connect, read some data, and then close but don't destroy every reference to the consumer, it will continue issuing metadata requests every five minutes.

I'm assuming this is because you are calling `rd_kafka_consumer_close` in the python `close()` method, but not calling `rd_kafka_destroy` until the consumer object is deallocated. It seems like once you call `close()`, the `Consumer` instance cannot be used for anything - is there a reason not to call `rd_kafka_destroy` in `close()`?

Description
===========
My consumer is not receiving any messages published to Kafka. Messages are being published from the same host where the consumer is running. The producer is using the same version of the library as the consumer (0.11.4). I am publishing to a Dockerized version of Kakfa using the official Confluent images. I have tried several versions of those images including 3.3.1, 4.0.0, and 4.1.0. I am able to read the messages using other means (including kafkacat).

issue_title: Producer fails to use Kafka cluster's fault tolerance?
issue_body:
I have three brokers functioning as a Kafka cluster, so that my 'bootstrap.servers' is "198.18.130.12,198.18.130.13,198.18.130.14". It happens that of these, 198.18.130.14 is down (even though 198.18.130.12 and 198.18.130.13 are still up). I would expect that because Kafka is smart about redundancy within a cluster, I would still be able to talk to the two brokers that are still up, but the producer seems to really want to connect to the down broker, and only the down broker, as far as I can tell.

I get a call to my error_cb():
code: -195, name: _TRANSPORT, str: 198.18.130.14:9092/bootstrap: Connect to ipv4#198.18.130.14:9092 failed: Connection refused

Then eventually I get a call to my on_delivery callback:
code: -192, name: _MSG_TIMED_OUT

So it looks like my producer is dead in the water.

Is there something I should be doing differently in the configuration?

FWIW, the corresponding consumer also gets a -195/_TRANSPORT/"198.18.130.14:9092/bootstrap: Connect to ipv4#198.18.130.14:9092 failed: Connection refused" error, but I have a hunch that it does not regard this as a showstopper the way the producer does.

**Python version**: any
**Library version**: any
**Description**

Would be awesome to keep a CHANGELOG file with changes between releases so we don't need to compare tags with github (like in https://github.com/Adyen/adyen-python-api-library/compare/1.1.0...1.2.0)

Seems that for some time we've been listing the maintainer in the `author` field of setup.py, but there's a separate `maintainer` field for that.

Current author metadata should be moved to `maintainer`, and `author` should be restored to their original values ([here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/commit/40996e01996096f252ef9d7754edf054e691900d#diff-2eeaed663bd0d25b7e608891384b7298L22)).

This is mostly a reminder to myself to do this, was thinking I'd do that and add a CONTRIBUTORS file as well, since the LICENSE should probably cover the contributors.

@niemeyer I'm assuming no objections to restoring your proper place in the metadata?

With #409 merged, the usage of `mstridx` is now uniform.  There are three places with:

```
ymd.append(value)
assert mstridx == -1
mstridx = len(ymd)-1
```

I propose that `mstridx` should be an attribute of `_ymd`.  My preferred implementation is a new `_ymd` method that gets called in some cases instead of `append`:

```
   def set_month(self, value, idx):
           assert self.mstridx is None # or -1 in in the current usage
           self.append(value)
           self.mstridx = len(self) - 1
           self._midx = idx  # This is new, will be discussed below
```

Then in `_parse`, the append + assert + set-mstridx becomes `ymd.set_month(value, i)`.  Advantages:

- One fewer state variable to carry around the `_parse` loop. This will make it simpler to refactor that into smaller functions.
- `_ymd.resolve_ymd` no longer needs `mstridx` as an argument.
- Analogous methods for `set_year` and `set_day` allow for similar validation (e.g. calling `ymd.append(info.convertyear(...))` to implicitly specify `ystridx`)

The new attribute `_midx`, and the analogous `_yidx, _didx` are there so that we can recover information later.  Keeping track of absolute position in addition to relative position may be important in cases where we need to back-track for disambiguation (see #394).  This may also be helpful for addressing #125.


Related: instead of carrying around `skipped_tokens` and `last_skipped_token_i`, use a set `skipped_indices`.  The re-combining can be done at the end of the parsing, `last_skipped_token_i` is easy to get, and we retain information that can be useful in potential post-processing.

issue_title: _fold attribute has inverted sense of PEP-495
issue_body:
See discussion on #57. The `_fold` attribute should have the inverted sense.

This may need to be a breaking change, as the default value for `fold` is 0, meaning that, in Python 3.6:

``` python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from dateutil.tz import gettz

LA = gettz('US/Pacific')
t = datetime(2014, 11, 2, 1, tzinfo=LA)
```

`t` should be interpreted as the _first_ 1AM, not the _second_ 1 AM.  This is the opposite of the assumption that has been made in every `tzinfo` subclass provided by `dateutil` up until this point.

Given that we've been pretty bad about ambiguous time zone support, hopefully no one is relying on this specific behavior, and the breaking change won't be so bad.

issue_title: In GAE zoneinfo.__init__.py fails when importing from subprocess
issue_body:
When running `dateutil` in GAE's sandbox, `zoneinfo.__init__.py` is failing on `from subprocess import check_call`.
`subprocess` module is overriden in GAE's sandbox.
If I move the `import` inside the function that uses it and wrap it with `try ... catch` it solves the problem.
Does this solution makes sense? If so I'll open a PR.

Alex

In `datetime`, it is possible to divide two `timedelta`s:

```python
import datetime
print(datetime.timedelta(1) / datetime.timedelta(2))  # output: 05
```

However the same is not possible in `dateutil` with `relativedelta`:

```python
import dateutil.relativedelta
dateutil.relativedelta.relativedelta(days=1) / dateutil.relativedelta.relativedelta(days=2)
# TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for /: 'relativedelta' and 'relativedelta'
```

I feel it would be good to include this functionality in `dateutil`. Thoughts?

issue_title: Choropleth slivers at low zoom levels
issue_body:
When using the `Map.choropleth` method my output has slivers present between features. See http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/gist/anonymous/1b85bccb47ea6a418591ef27c3f2bc5e for an example.

I've put this down to Leaflet applying a smoothing algorithm to the features as per http://gis.stackexchange.com/questions/143449/geojson-rendering-in-leaflet.

Adding `{smoothFactor: 0}` (a `PolyLine` option) to `L.geoJson` removes these from the map at all zoom levels. I can't see a way to use this with the current `Map.choropleth` method. Am I missing something? 

From what I can see, I could accomplish this using the `GeoJson` class and a style function as a workaround for now.

We can use the `Vega` class to add vincent popups (see cell [6] of the notebookbelow), but we lost the auto padding

```python
if isinstance(vega.padding, dict):
    width += vega.padding['left']+vega.padding['right']
    height += vega.padding['top']+vega.padding['bottom']
else:
    width += 75
    height += 50
```

We also lost the ability to pass arbitrary HTML as a popup.  (Cells [5] and [7].)

@BibMartin vincent is not that healthy, so I am thinking that we can substitute the Vega class for something more generic.  Does this make sense to you?  Or or you have a different idea?

issue_title: <get> in Previous with UTF-8 mode enabled
issue_body:
Hello,

Consider this simple example. (with the UTF-8 mode enabled)

```
+ i am *
- <set name=<star>> You are <get name>.

+ no
% you are <get name>
- Ok.
```

It works with

```
- I am Jim.
- You are Jim.
- No.
- Ok.
```

But does not works with

```
- I am Léon
- You are Léon.
- No.
No response found
```

I know that UTF-8 mode

> is considered an experimental feature because all of the edge cases of supporting Unicode haven't been fully tested.

But it is the first trouble we got with the utf-8 mode so I report it.

## Description
CODESIZE uses the number of instructions versus their actual size. EXTCODESIZE doesn't use a concrete number atm which it should.
@LoCorVin reported https://github.com/b-mueller/laser-ethereum/issues/41 in the laser repository.


**implementation**
To fix this problem both modules will need some way of accessing the bytecode of a contract to calculate the size in bytes, or some way to access it directly. And have the instruction implementation use that value
Hi, Need to now if tastypie latest version is supporting Django 1.11?

issue_title: support loading custom jars and python-api.zip for python via pip install
issue_body:
When user define new criterions, layers and wrap it in python, it requires loading external jar/python-api.zip when jupyter/BigDL starts up.  But pip install doesn't allow loading customized jars/zip now. External jars can only be loaded using script to launch jupyter. 

We need 

- Support loading custom jars and python-api.zip after pip installation of BigDL
- It's better to have external jars/zip loaded dynamically with a command (which can be executed inside jupyter) or an api.  It won't be convinient if it requires frequent install/uninstall.

```
And now I'm getting NoReverseMatch in certain templates... Commenting that line out brings everything back to normal. How could this be?

The ``PVSystem`` class and the ``system_def`` function accept keyword arguments ``series_modules`` and ``parallel_modules``. Until PR #171, pvlib code did not actually use them and they only existed for user extensions. As brought up in #171 by @jforbess, these names are confusing at best.

Now is a good time to change them since they didn't actually do anything before. I would support something like ``series_modules --> modules_per_string`` and ``parallel_modules --> strings``. 

I'd normally wait for 0.4 to implement a change that could break user code, but in this case I'm leaning towards 0.3.3 release because 1) it's unlikely that anyone is actually using these arguments and 2) #171 is going in 0.3.3.

Thoughts?

fixing #181 led me to a few more problems with our setup.py file:

1. AUTHORS should be something like 'PVLIB Python developers' instead of naming individuals.
2. The current LONG DESCRIPTION results in a poorly formatted PyPI page. Probably fix this after #182.
2. The version check can be deleted. It's not helping anyone.
2. DESCRIPTION has a typo 'set functions' --> 'set of functions'
2. Move 'install_requires' from setuptools_kwargs to explicit kwarg
2. Add 'tests_require' = ['nose'] kwarg
2. LICENSE doesn't need 'license'

issue_title: Provider extension not working as expected
issue_body:
Executing the example found [here](http://python-dependency-injector.ets-labs.org/providers/custom.html#writing-of-custom-providers), I get

`Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "injector.py", line 36, in <module>
    user1 = users_factory()
  File "src/dependency_injector/providers.pyx", line 97, in dependency_injector.providers.Provider.__call__ (src/dependency_injector/providers.c:2652)
  File "src/dependency_injector/providers.pyx", line 197, in dependency_injector.providers.Provider._provide (src/dependency_injector/providers.c:4580)
NotImplementedError`

as if the `_provide` method was not overridden. The peculiar behaviour is that if I explicitly call `users_factory._provide()` it works as expected, if I call `users_factory()` (which implies passing through the `__call__` method) it raises `NotImplemented`. For instance, in the following code, when I call `users_factory._provide()` it prints `test`, when I call `users_factory()` it raises `NotImplemented`.

```import dependency_injector.providers as providers


class User(object):
    """Example class User."""


class UsersFactory(providers.Provider):
    """Example users factory."""

    __slots__ = ('_factory',)

    def __init__(self):
        """Initializer."""
        self._factory = providers.Factory(User)
        super(UsersFactory, self).__init__()

    def _provide(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """Return provided instance.

        :param args: tuple of context positional arguments
        :type args: tuple[object]

        :param kwargs: dictionary of context keyword arguments
        :type kwargs: dict[str, object]

        :rtype: object
        """
        print('test')
        return self._factory(*args, **kwargs)


# Users factory:
users_factory = UsersFactory()

users_factory._provide()
# Creating several User objects:
user1 = users_factory()
user2 = users_factory()

# Making some asserts:
assert isinstance(user1, User)
assert isinstance(user2, User)
assert user1 is not user2
```

issue_title: Can I use two exploration_techniques at the same time?
issue_body:
I checked the source code of explore in pathgroup.

```
    def explore(self, stash=None, n=None, find=None, avoid=None, find_stash='found', avoid_stash='avoid', cfg=None, num_find=1, step_func=None):
        """
        Tick stash "stash" forward (up to "n" times or until "num_find" paths are found), looking for condition "find",
        avoiding condition "avoid". Stores found paths into "find_stash' and avoided paths into "avoid_stash".

        The "find" and "avoid" parameters may be any of:

        - An address to find
        - A set or list of addresses to find
        - A function that takes a path and returns whether or not it matches.

        If an angr CFG is passed in as the "cfg" parameter and "find" is either a number or a list or a set, then
        any paths which cannot possibly reach a success state without going through a failure state will be
        preemptively avoided.
        """
        num_find += len(self.stashes[find_stash]) if find_stash in self.stashes else 0
        tech = exploration_techniques.Explorer(find=find,
                                   avoid=avoid,
                                   find_stash=find_stash,
                                   avoid_stash=avoid_stash,
                                   cfg=cfg,
                                   num_find=num_find)
        self.use_technique(tech)
        out = self.run(stash=stash,
                       step_func=step_func,
                       n=n)
        out.remove_tech(tech)
        self.remove_tech(tech)
        return out
```

I find it uses _angr.exploration_techniques.explorer.Explorer_,but I want to use _angr.exploration_techniques.looplimiter.LoopLimiter_ at the same time. How can I make it?

issue_title: Statically following a variable in a program
issue_body:
Given the very simple case of a larger problem:

```
#pragma optimize("", off)
void main()
{
	int *x;
	*x = 24;
	malloc(*x);
}
```
Which compiles to:
![idard](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23474424/43841013-cf4f5d92-9b2a-11e8-9086-6ce4b654a574.PNG)

I want to find the size of the allocation at 0x1045C (which storted at R0 up to that point).  
Using Reaching Definitions I can follow R0 to be defined by `R3` in 0x10458, and then to follow R3 to be defined at 0x10454 by `[R3]`.
However, from there, things become difficult - using Reaching Definitions I can't find any way to make the next step to 0x1044c, where `[R3]` is assigned. 

Do you have any idea of how can I connect line 0x10454 with 0x1044c, using Reaching Definitions or any other method?
Or generally any other idea how can I find with angr the source of the allocation at 0x1045C?

issue_title: False memory read on realloc passing NULL as a pointer
issue_body:
Hello,

I've found some different behavior on the realloc function usage and angr realloc implementation.
According to https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/memory/realloc

`If ptr is NULL, the behavior is the same as calling malloc(new_size).`
So in case realloc is being like so:
```
void realloc_null() {
        int* p = realloc(NULL, 0x20);
}
```

Memory breakpoint will happen on the address of 0 (NULL) although we shouldn't read from the buffer received.

This is the realloc implementation: https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/procedures/libc/realloc.py

I will create pull request fixing this issue.

issue_title: Deprecate the use of Basic Authentication in the examples
issue_body:
Originally reported at https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/issues/590

Currently, the demo instances and the code in the tutorial are relying on Basic Authentication. We should get rid of these examples and let the users directly rely on OAuth providers instead.

The user id should be unique, and the way to do this is to rely to an external authn provider, and all of the services we integrate with are integrable by using OAuth.

We should make it really easy to have the examples integrate with Github / Twitter / Facebook authentication. For instance, having them defined as extra-dependencies could make sense. Consider:

`$ pip install kinto[github-auth]`

issue_body:
This is a verification for [Issue 2527](https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2527). This change should allow pulp smash to show the same results in an environment like this which will come with F26:

```
amqp==2.1.4
billiard==3.5.0.2
celery==4.0.2
kombu==4.0.2+a patch to be released via RPM in Fedora
vine==1.1.3
```

Versus a typical environment today on other distros:


```
amqp==1.4.9
billiard==3.3.0.23
celery==3.1.20
kombu==3.0.33
```

To test this we use Pulp smash by running them on both environments.

Smash an F26 Environment
------------------------------------

1. On a fresh rpm installation in an F26 environment, ensure the following packages are installed via RPM and come from the Fedora base or updates repos: amqp, billiard, celery, kombu, and vine. This should deliver the "new" dependency stack.
2. Run pulp-smash and record any failures

Smash a F25 Environment
----------------------------------
1. This should have the old dependency stack with an rpm based install.
2. Run pulp-smash and record any failures.

issue_title: Test publish yum repo with different checksums
issue_body:
1. create an "rpm" repo in pulp with one or more RPMs, SRPMs, and DRPMs.
2. publish it with the "sha256" checksum type
3. verify publish (see below)
4. publish it with the "sha1" checksum type
5. verify publish (see below)

### Verification
It depends on how fancy and robust you want to get when it comes to inspecting the published XML. You'll need to somehow look at primary, other, filelists, and prestodelta XML files and make sure all of the unit-related checksums are using the checksum type you asked for.

Smash could download the files and actually parse them as XML. That wouldn't be too difficult, but who likes parsing XML...

Smash could just ensure that the string "sha256" shows up in the case when it should, and doesn't when it shouldn't.

In the case of "other" and "filelists", the checksum type itself won't be included. Each package will have a ``pkgid`` attribute that is the checksum value of the specified type. You could just verify that it's the correct length. Or you could actually cross-reference it with the checksum for the corresponding package listed in primary.

Another general way to help with this verification is to just make sure yum and/or dnf can actually read the metadata and use it. In my manual testing, I published a rhel7 kickstart repo and kickstarted a VM from it, just to make sure it was generally sane.

issue_title: What is the best way to ensure that a composite ID is unique?
issue_body:
If we have a type which has a composite key, what is the best way to define it in Graphene?

Should serialize it ourselves?  Like:

```
   self.id = '{}/{}'.format(id_a, id_b)
```

Or should we let Graphene serialize an object for us?
```
   self.id = {
      'a': id_a,
      'b': id_b,
   }
```

Or should we do something different?

Is there a benefit to either approach?  We're inclined to go with our own custom key because we think it is most predictable... but we weren't sure if there is a convention which is preferred over custom serialization of composite keys.  

Thoughts?

issue_title: is_leaf_type() returns True when the type is List of String
issue_body:
```
In [1]: from graphql import GraphQLString, GraphQLList

In [2]: from graphql.type.definition import is_leaf_type

In [3]: is_leaf_type(GraphQLList(GraphQLString))
Out[3]: True
```

See the current `master` implementation [here](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/cfeaa393259266572e634f6e7e8dfd4fc591b2a4/graphql/type/definition.py#L43): `is_leaf_type()` calls `get_named_type()`, which unwinds both lists and non-nulls to get at the inner type. The list unwinding is incorrect in this instance, as a list of strings is clearly a non-leaf type.

issue_title: Multiple calls to annotate returns only latest annotation when calling select_subclasses.
issue_body:
## Problem

When making chained calls to `annotate()` on a single queryset, only the last annotation applied is returned after calling select_subclasses. If you don't call select_subclasses, you do receive all annotations.

## Environment

- Django Model Utils version: 3.1.1
- Django version: 2.0
- Python version: 3.5.2
- Other libraries used, if any: n/a

## Code examples

In the sample below, the only annotation that is available in the evaluated queryset is `correct_count`.

```
import django
django.setup()  # noqa

from django.db.models import Case, When, ExpressionWrapper, FloatField, Sum, Q

from sampleapp import models

correct_count_calc = ExpressionWrapper(
    Sum(
        Case(
            When((Q(answer__correct=True) & Q(answer__quizresult__user__is_superuser=False)), then=1),
            default=0
        )
    ), FloatField()
)

answered_count_calc = ExpressionWrapper(
    Sum(
        Case(
            When(answer__quizresult__user__is_superuser=False, then=1.0),
            default=0
        )
    ), FloatField()
)

q = models.BaseQuestion.objects.annotate(answered_count=answered_count_calc) \
                               .annotate(correct_count=correct_count_calc).select_subclasses().first()
print(q.__dict__)
```
It seems the problem stems from the snippet below, which sets (rather than appending to) the `qset._annotated` value for each subsequent call to `.annotate()`.

https://github.com/jazzband/django-model-utils/blob/1eff6d0d8f5e880db63493b59126a448a30a74e1/model_utils/managers.py#L116

I have a simple fix for this issue here:

https://github.com/digismack/django-model-utils/commit/6a72cebfc02fd19e68c15343fa9647256ad1e0f9

I was unable to get the test suite to run. They all fail with `AppRegistryNotReady: Apps aren't loaded yet.` If someone would be willing to help me out a bit, I'd love to submit a pull request with tests and such.

issue_body:

issue_title: How to show the loss curve of training set and validation set at the same time using the customed estimator?
issue_body:
Hi, recently i customed estimator api to build regression model.  In order to clear out the changes of loss value in the training set and validation set. I need to know that how to show the loss curve of training and validation set at the same time. I tried to use train_and_evaluate api of estimator and i got the following picture.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19348442/39224762-76df9b92-487b-11e8-888d-b64825e99c82.png)
As it show, the result of evaluation is a point, but i want a line like the loss curve of training set.
Here is my code:
`from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division
from __future__ import print_function

import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np
import import_tanmiao
import plot_util

STEPS = 1000
fig_savepath = "F:/machine_learning_flow_pred_laoyu_tanmiao/tf_projects/test/models/images/"


def my_dnn_regression_fn(features, labels, mode, params):
    """ A model function implementing DNN regression for a custom Estimator """

    # Extract the input intp  a dense layer, according to feature_columns.
    top = tf.feature_column.input_layer(features, params['feature_columns'])

    # Iterate over the "hidden_units" list of layer size, default is [20].
    for units in params.get('hidden_units', [20]):
        # Add a hidden layer, densely connected on top of previous layer.
        top = tf.layers.dense(inputs=top, units=units, activation=tf.nn.relu)

    # Connect a liner output layer on top.
    output_layer = tf.layers.dense(inputs=top, units=1)

    output_layer = tf.cast(output_layer, tf.float64)
    # Reshape the output layer to a 1-deim Tensor to return predictions
    predictions = tf.squeeze(output_layer, 1)

    if mode == tf.estimator.ModeKeys.PREDICT:
        # In 'PREDICT' mode we only need to return predictions.
        return tf.estimator.EstimatorSpec(
            mode=mode, predictions={"predictions": predictions})

    # calculate the loss using mean squared error
    average_loss = tf.losses.mean_squared_error(labels, predictions)

    # Pre-made estimators use the total_loss instead of the average,
    # so report total_loss for compatibility.
    batch_size = tf.shape(labels)[0]
    total_loss = tf.to_float(batch_size) * average_loss

    if mode == tf.estimator.ModeKeys.TRAIN:
        optimizer = params.get("optimizer", tf.train.AdamOptimizer)
        optimizer = optimizer(params.get("learning_rate", None))
        train_op = optimizer.minimize(
            loss=average_loss, global_step=tf.train.get_global_step())
        return tf.estimator.EstimatorSpec(
            mode=mode, loss=total_loss, train_op=train_op)

    # In the evaluation mode we will calculate evaluation metrics.
    assert mode == tf.estimator.ModeKeys.EVAL

    # Calculate root mean squared error
    rmse = tf.metrics.root_mean_squared_error(labels, predictions)

    # Add the rmse to collection of evaluation metrics.
    eval_metrics = {"rmse": rmse}

    return tf.estimator.EstimatorSpec(
        mode=mode,
        # Report sum of error for compatibility with pre-made estimators.
        loss=total_loss,
        eval_metric_ops=eval_metrics)


def main(argv):
    """ Builds, Trians, and evaluates the model. """
    assert len(argv) == 1

    # laod data from local disk.
    (x_train,
     y_train), (x_dev,
                y_dev), (x_test,
                         y_test) = import_tanmiao.load_data(logtrans=True)

    # Build the training input_fn
    def input_train(features=x_train, labels=y_train, batch_size=128):
        """ An input function for training """
        # convert the input to a Dataset
        dataset = tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices((dict(features), labels))

        # Shffling with a buffer larger than the data set ensures
        # that the examples are will mixed.
        dataset = dataset.shuffle(4000).batch(
            batch_size).repeat().make_one_shot_iterator().get_next()

        return dataset

    # Build the validation input_fn
    def input_dev(features=x_dev, labels=y_dev, batch_size=128):
        # Convert the input to a Dataset
        dataset = tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices((dict(features), labels))

        # Shuffling
        dataset = dataset.shuffle(2000).batch(
            batch_size).make_one_shot_iterator().get_next()

        return dataset

    feature_columns = [
        tf.feature_column.numeric_column("X1"),
        tf.feature_column.numeric_column("X2"),
        tf.feature_column.numeric_column("X3"),
        tf.feature_column.numeric_column("X4"),
        tf.feature_column.numeric_column("X5"),
        tf.feature_column.numeric_column("X6"),
        tf.feature_column.numeric_column("X7")
    ]

    # Set learning_rate decay
    # global_step_ = tf.Variable(0)
    # learning_rate = tf.train.exponential_decay(
    #     0.1, global_step_, 100, 0.96, staircase=True)

    # Build a custom Estimator, using the model_fn
    # 'params' is passed through the 'model_fn'
    model = tf.estimator.Estimator(
        model_fn=my_dnn_regression_fn,
        model_dir=
        "F:/machine_learning_flow_pred_laoyu_tanmiao/tf_projects/test/models/temp2.2",
        params={
            'feature_columns': feature_columns,
            'learning_rate': 0.1,
            'optimizer': tf.train.AdamOptimizer,
            'hidden_units': [20, 20, 20, 20]
        })

    # use tf.estimator.train_and_evaluate
    train_spec = tf.estimator.TrainSpec(input_fn=input_train,max_steps=10000)
    eval_spec = tf.estimator.EvalSpec(input_fn=input_dev,steps=10000,throttle_secs=60,start_delay_secs=0)
    tf.estimator.train_and_evaluate(model, train_spec, eval_spec)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # The estimator periodically generates "INFO" logs; make this logs visable
    tf.logging.set_verbosity(tf.logging.INFO)
    tf.app.run(main=main)`
How can I display loss curve both for training and validation set simultaneously?

issue_title: Audio thumbnails/summaries in the embedding visualization
issue_body:
Migrated from https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/7106

> We (@pfcm and I) have been working with audio data (bird songs) and would like a way to inspect (via listening) embeddings of small chunks of audio files. The main use would be to help us to validate and explore our data. In our setting we have few labels, so we are especially interested in using it to inspect clusters and validate (with humans) their accuracy/reliability/clusteryness. 
> 
> We would like to know;
> * Are you guys planning on adding this sort of feature to tensorflow? If so, can we help to speed it up? 
> * If we were do it how would you recommend that we implement it so that it is supported in the future?

> We would like audio summaries to work with the embedding visualiser. Much like there are thumbnails of each mnist image (see link), we would like to be able to click on an embedded datapoint; to listen to it (currently we are just using the spectrograms as image thumbnails) and to figure out where it came from (which part of which audio recording).

[Docu for lambda@edge](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-edge.html)

Some things that have to be considered are that the trigger can only be applied to a replica version of that lambda in us-east-1. After the trigger has been applied that lambda replica will be distributed to the edge locations of the cloudfront.

issue_title: Extend a driver and reference it with napalm-ansible
issue_body:
As discussed in [115](https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm-ansible/issues/115) I am bringing this topic to a broader audience. 

I am trying to write some custom methods for my project; e.g. get_service_policies(). 

I have created in my working directory the **custom_napalm/** folder with **ios.py** and **__init__.py** ; ios.py extends the IOSDriver adding the desired method as explained in [doc]( http://napalm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/extend_driver.html)

Everything works fine when using this method from the main python script (run in the working directory).

What I am struggling to achieve is to refer this method in some ansible playbook using the napalm-ansible module. 

I understand the task has to be written as follows:

```
- name: get facts from device
  connection : local
  napalm_get_facts:
    hostname: "localhost"
    optional_args: {'port' : "{{local_port}}" }
    username: "{{ ansible_user }}"
    dev_os: "{{ os }}"
    password: "{{ ansible_password }}"
    filter: ["service_policies"]
  register: result
```

The problem is that the ansible module napalm_get_facts.py is executed in some tmp folder without the custom_napalm folder and that's why is not possible to find ios.py.

If I add directly the method to the napalm/ios.py file it works from the ansible module as well.

issue_title: Combining Classification and Regression with tflearn
issue_body:
As the topic says, I want to combine a classification and a regression problem.
I want to have 9 output neurons. 7 of them from the softmax activation and two of them from the linear activation. I also want to give them a different loss function.  How can I go about doing that?

```
net = tflearn.global_avg_pool(net)
# Regression
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 9,activation='softmax')

```
The following obviously did not work as I was unable to add the outputs from the two parts of the network to a single regression layer.

```
net1 = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 7,activation='softmax')
net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer=opt,
                         loss='categorical_crossentropy')
net2 = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 2,activation='linear')
net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer=opt,
                         loss='mean_square')
net = net1+net2
```
Any ideas/help is appreciated.

issue_title: Too much data transfer when logging to lots of environments
issue_body:
I'm often running experiments where ~20 environments are actively updated, with ~6 plots in each. My logging frequency is very high --- about 3 times a second for each environment.

Previously, visdom's client side would only retrieve data whenever it needed to render --- i.e., when selecting an environment from the dropdown, only its data would be downloaded.

I think that at some point in the past ~3 months (I could be wrong), visdom underwent a frontend update such that data from all environments is downloaded client-side all the time.
This update makes it impossible to use the web UI, as everything is frozen due to high CPU usage (presumably from downloading and de-serializing the many streams of data being logged).

issue_title: Making all the word vector weights positive
issue_body: Can we have an option to make all the learned  weights as positive only cause a class of  clustering algorithms expect only positive numbers as weights and hence cannot be applied on the same

I am implementing a Rust library that can read and use fastText embeddings (so that I don't have to bind against the C++ library). While writing unit tests, I found that fastText uses the FNV-1a hash incorrectly (I think). FNV hashes per octet of data [1], however, the following line in `Dictionary::hash`:

~~~c++
h = h ^ uint32_t(str[i]);
~~~

Sign-extends any byte > 0x7f (thus leading and continuation bytes in UTF-8), since `char` is signed in C++. Consequently, the 24 most significant bits of the FNV state get flipped. The following should be correct:

~~~C++
h = h ^ uint32_t(uint8_t(str[i]));
~~~

I am not sending a pull request, since it would make all existing models incompatible, and it will probably not lead to any measurable differences. But I thought it would be good to mention this in case anyone else runs into this.

[1] http://www.isthe.com/chongo/tech/comp/fnv/index.html#FNV-1a

From https://github.com/pyFFTW/pyFFTW/issues/106#issuecomment-322802960

Ingredients:

- OS Linux 4.4.0-92, Ubuntu):

- conda base packages (miniconda):
>     numpy 1.13.1 py36_0
>     python 3.6.2 0
>     mkl 2017.0.3 0

- pyFFTW compiled via pip:
>     pyFFTW (0.10.4)

- pyFFTW installed from conda-forge:
>     pyfftw 0.10.4 np113py36_1 conda-forge


## **The problem**: numpy is faster than conda-forge pyFFTW!

    Numpy (with mkl)
```
In [1]: import numpy as np
In [2]: x=np.random.random(4096)
In [3]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
79.1 µs ± 661 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

    Locally compiled pyFFTW from pip, works great:
```
In [6]: import pyfftw
In [7]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [8]: a[:] = x
In [9]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [10]: %timeit fftobj()
15.4 µs ± 443 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

BTW, it speeds up substantially also with interfaces, and I didn't even switch on the cache.

    conda-forge pyFFTW
```
In [1]: import numpy as np
In [2]: import pyfftw
In [3]: x=np.random.random(4096)
In [4]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [5]: a[:] = x
In [6]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [7]: %timeit fftobj()
110 µs ± 202 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
In [8]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
77.9 µs ± 73.2 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

   conda-forge pyFFTW, reordering imports:
```
In [1]: import pyfftw
In [2]: import numpy as np
In [3]: x=np.random.random(4096)
In [4]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [5]: a[:] = x
In [6]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [7]: %timeit fftobj()
111 µs ± 427 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

   conda-forge pyFFTW, MKL-unsupported transform:
```
In [1]: import pyfftw
In [2]: import numpy as np
In [3]: shape = (1, 1, 4096)
In [4]: x=np.random.random(np.prod(shape)).reshape(shape)
In [5]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [6]: a[:] = x
In [7]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [9]: %timeit fftobj()
116 µs ± 5.72 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
In [10]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
78 µs ± 730 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

   same on a larger array:
```
In [9]: shape = (100, 1, 4096)
In [10]: x=np.random.random(np.prod(shape)).reshape(shape)
In [11]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [12]: a[:] = x
In [13]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
7.87 ms ± 9.75 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
In [14]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [15]: %timeit fftobj()
11.3 ms ± 12.2 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
```

So, at the moment, I am actually using pyFFTW from pip (locally compiled), and use it for 1D transforms (or 2D over 2 axes, etc), whereas I use numpy.fft for other transforms.
Similarly to #92, arrays with `flags.writeable=False` should be handled properly by `builders` and also by the `FFTW` class.

Currently the only way to retrieve a transaction is by ID. However there is no way to retrieve a transaction ownership chain using the driver.

BigchainDB already has a get_transactions_filtered() method but doesn't allow both CREATE and TRANSFER operations to be searched at the same time. issue_title: csvlook 1.0 unable to read from stdin, raises error "underlying stream is not seekable"
issue_body:
In csvkit 0.9.1, you could do this:

```sh
$ csvcut -c 1,2,3 examples/iris.csv | csvlook
|---------------+-------------+---------------|
|  sepal_length | sepal_width | petal_length  |
|---------------+-------------+---------------|
|  5.1          | 3.5         | 1.4           |
|  4.9          | 3.0         | 1.4           |
|  4.7          | 3.2         | 1.3           |
|  4.6          | 3.1         | 1.5           |
```

In 1.0, this error is raised: `underlying stream is not seekable`. This seems like it would be expected behavior for `csvsort`, but doesn't seem necessary for `csvlook`:

https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/blob/master/csvkit/utilities/csvsort.py#L31

(actually, is it necessary for csvsort? Doesn't csvstat essentially require the entire input to be read before it can do its aggregation? I guess that can be saved for another issue/feature request)

issue_title: SendGrid smtp-id not always matching anymail_status
issue_body:
ESP: SendGrid
django-anymail: 2.2
Python: 2.7
Django: 1.11.10

Just after we make the `msg = AnymailMessage(**kwargs)` call we store the data from `msg.anymail_status` in our database for tracking. The issue we're seeing is that the SendGrid webhook will often times return POST data with a `message_id` and `esp_event['smtp-id`] that do not match the values Anymail generated for us.

As an example:
We send a message and receive an smtp-id of `
<20180523175141.98405.51520@example.com>` in the `msg.anymail_status`.
When the webhook communicates updates to us we find that the `message_id` for the same message is `<YTJPMXaQSY-KzuEUmXrDWg@ismtpd0025p1las1.sendgrid.net>` which means that we are unable to identify which message this belongs to.

Here's the code that calls AnymailMessage:
```python
text_message = render_to_string(templates['text'], context)
html_message = render_to_string(templates['html'], context)
msg = AnymailMessage(
    subject=templates['subject'],
    body=text_message,
    to=[user.email],
)
msg.attach_alternative(html_message, "text/html")
msg.send()

# store the anymail status data to our database for tracking via the webhook
Email().create_from_anymail(msg.anymail_status, Email.INVITATION)
```

The `Email()` model holds the `create_from_anymail(anymail_status, event)` method where event is an optional custom tracker for the event triggering the email send (unrelated to anymail):
```python
@classmethod
def create_from_anymail(cls, anymail_status, event=None):
    for recipient in anymail_status.recipients:
        kwargs = {
            'to_email': recipient,
            'status': anymail_status.recipients[recipient].status,
            'message_id': anymail_status.recipients[recipient].message_id,
            'response': anymail_status.esp_response
        }

        if event:
            kwargs['event'] = event

        email = Email(**kwargs)
        email.save()
```

From what I can gather from the SendGrid webhook documentation the `smtp-id` should be returned for every webhook POST and is `a unique ID attached to the message by the originating system`.

I've also contacted SendGrid support but wanted to present the issue here in case anyone has seen something similar and may have ideas on how to get a consistent identifier from our system into SendGrid.

issue_title: Feature request: requests per-request timing metric customisation
issue_body:
Talisker will emit a statsd metric for every inter-service HTTP request made with `talisker.requests`. However, the metric name is calculated using the URL used in the request. The issue I'm facing is that we use an internal IP for the remote service rather than a domain name (in this case the target service has no domain name, and we don't want to add one).

Initially I thought that this meant I probably couldn't use talisker's per-request metrics feature. However, I figured I'd open this bug to see if there's a viable workaround.

Perhaps we could have an alternative interface in `talisker.requests` that allows a caller to pass a service name, which talisker could then use instead of the IP address when emitting the metric?

I tried to use letsencrypt-auto in order to renew the certificates but it seems that the new certificates are generated in a new directory ` /etc/letsencrypt/live/<domain>-0001` and I do not want this, I just want to replace the old certificate with the current one and to reload them after this. 

I don't want to have to change the nginx configuration path each time I run letsencrypt-auto, probably from inside a cron job.

issue_title: certbot-apache broken on Gentoo: incorrect os info parsing?
issue_body:
In certbot-apache's constants.py, the client expects `"gentoo base system"` as key for the OS-specific defaults. However, `os_info[0]` is `"gentoo"`. As a result, certbot-apache fails with "Unable to find Apache version" since it relies on the non-working debian defaults.

I am not sure, but I feel that "gentoo base system" is a leftover from letsencrypt-auto which tested for distribution-specific files, such as `/etc/gentoo-release` which indeed contains that string. In contrast, certbot seems to rely on `/etc/os-release`, which only mentions "gentoo".

Therefore, I suggest that "gentoo base system" be changed to simply "gentoo". At least it fixes the problem for me.

issue_body:
While Augeas saved us writing a grammar for Apache config files, it has caused us a lot of other issues. Off the top of my head:

hercules-team/augeas#423
hercules-team/augeas#435
hercules-team/augeas#466
hercules-team/augeas#467
hercules-team/augeas#470
hercules-team/python-augeas#15
hercules-team/python-augeas#21
and recently hercules-team/python-augeas#30

There are also well over a dozen bugs that have been found in their Apache configuration parsing that have subsequently been fixed. While upstream has always been willing to help (and their help has been greatly appreciated), we continue to have issues, some of which are quite serious. Even worse, issues like hercules-team/python-augeas#15 have sat around for over a year with no signs of progress.

While Augeas has been useful for us, I think it's time we investigate finding another library for parsing Apache configuration files. Ideally, this library should not be reliant on any non-Python libraries or programs as this dramatically simplifies packaging.

issue_title: Certbot and --apache modules looks for vhost conf files in sites-available causing missconfigurations in edge cases
issue_body:
This will cause the configuration to be written to the wrong conf file 

>>>if more than one vhost file has the same servername specified<<<

repro steps

- in apache2 create 2 vhosts*80 files in sites-available, give theme the same server name, enable the one that is alpahabetiacally last ( Avhost.conf and Bvhost.conf, enable Bvhost)

- run certbot; (sudo letsencrypt --apache -d commonname.com -d www.commonname.com ) 

configurations will be written to the first conf, which is dissabled and the resulting -le-ssl conf file in sites enabled is name based off the dissabled configuration (Avhost.conf has redirect rules, and Avhost-le-ssl.conf is available )

Potential fix-

only look for vhosts conf files present in sites-enabled when using the apache plugin unless otherwise specified

issue_title: Tags too short or with special characters silently prevent from submitting a dataset
issue_body:
Environment:
- I run the server in a local development environment
- I use the branch `dev`

Steps:
- I submit a dataset
- In the step 2, I set the the mandatory fields
- In the step 2, I set a tag which contains special characters (`aaa$`) or is shorter than 2 characters (`a`)
- I click on `Next`

Unwanted behaviour:
- I get stuck

Explanation:
- On the [server side](https://github.com/opendatateam/udata/blob/dev/udata/forms/fields.py#L309), I got a validation error, which leads the browser to fail silently.

Possible solutions:
- Prevent the user from entering a tag which does not match what is expected by the server
- Convert the tags on the client side so that they match what is expected by the server
- Convert the tags on the server side so that they match what is expected by the server
- Display an error message on the client side explaining that the tags are not well formatted
- Live with this bug until we remove WTForms (since it seems to be related to the WTForms machinery)

It is currently the date of creation instead of publication while the label is about publication.

### Solution

- allow edit of date
- update date of creation when switch from draft to published

issue_title: Create end to end API tests
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

At the moment the API endpoints are only [unit tested](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/c3c18e31b566f1c209051cdafc64c5fb1219ca4f/raiden/tests/api/test_api.py) and we don't have any end to end tests for the API.

## Solution

Create end to end tests using the API endpoints.
## Tasklist

issue_title: Distribute compiled ABI to end users
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

In light of #179 we should get rid of the user dependency for `solc`. While it is possible (see #421) to redistribute `solc`, we should avoid the need to update our releases whenever a new `solc` is released.
Also, compiling the ABI at the time of the program start adds unnecessary delay.

## Solution

I propose a solution that checks for each required ABI

- if a json-file of `<SOLIDITY_FILE>.json` is available AND
- if the sha1-checksum of the content of `<SOLIDITY_FILE>` matches the one recorded in `<SOLIDITY_FILE>.json::sha1_checksum`

and only compiles the ABI, if the file is not there or the checksum doesn't match.

A more involved approach would also include to
- record the compiler version at compile time and
- do a `semver` check against the user version and compile if user's `solc` is newer.
But after checking with solidity team I believe that this will only be necessary after major `solc` releases (since the ABI output is not expected to change).

## Tasklist
todo

`ImageFolder`'s `pil_loader` converts to RGB. This may be a standard for images, but I recently needed RGBA and was confused as to why it wasn't opening correctly (its not in the documentation that it does this, and there isnt an argument that makes it obvious or allows you to overwrite this behavior, you need to make your own loader). I propose we add an option to `ImageFolder` called `image_mode`, defaulted to "RGB", such that if this parameter is any value other than none that value is passed to `pil_loader`'s convert call, and if it is `None` it bypasses the convert function altogether.

We could probably just convert the [existing pretrained torch models](https://github.com/liuzhuang13/DenseNet).

Open to a PR?

Do you have any particular license under which the pretrained model files are released?

We want to modify (i.e. port to TensorFlow) and redistribute (i.e. use for [CS 231N](http://cs231n.stanford.edu/)) the weights files from this repo; is this permissible?

For covimerage it could be exposed through an override/monkeypatch for the xml command probably.

(didn't actually find any, but I looked)

### Description
The Crunchyroll plugin contains the following format string:
`API_USER_AGENT = "Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; iPhone OS 8.3.0; {})"`
which fails on Python 2.6, which requires format placeholders to be explicitly numbered. See https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/string.html#formatstrings : 

> Changed in version 2.7: The positional argument specifiers can be omitted, so '{} {}' is equivalent to '{0} {1}'.

### Expected / Actual behavior
Expected: Crunchyroll plugin is able to load stream
Actual:
```
>>> streamlink.streams("http://www.crunchyroll.com/jojos-bizarre-adventure/episode-1-the-man-possessed-by-an-evil-spirit-652155")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/streamlink/api.py", line 13, in streams
    return session.streams(url, **params)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/streamlink/session.py", line 423, in streams
    return plugin.streams(**params)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/streamlink/plugin/plugin.py", line 240, in streams
    raise PluginError(err)
streamlink.exceptions.PluginError: zero length field name in format
```

### Reproduction steps / Stream URLs to test
```
>>> import streamlink
>>> streamlink.streams("http://www.crunchyroll.com/jojos-bizarre-adventure/episode-1-the-man-possessed-by-an-evil-spirit-652155")
```
(Also fails on CLI)
My testing failed identically with or without my Crunchyroll credentials, so I believe they are not required to verify this.

### Environment details (operating system, python version, etc.)
CentOS 6, Python 2.6

> Python 2.6.6 (r266:84292, Aug 18 2016, 15:13:37)
> [GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-17)] on linux2

### Comments, logs, screenshots, etc.
I'll be submitting a (very trivial) pull request shortly. If I have time I'll go through some other plugins and see if they have similar issues.

Hello Team Django-HelpDesk
First of all , Thank you for publishing this work and making it available, open-source and free of charge.
I started using your code and i'm facing many -many- troubles, i don't know where to start actually.
What i aim by this comment is to help next releases designs decisions and implementation.

App Design:
* The architectural design have many assumptions built-in , like the actual use of local mail server, this becomes clear as queue mail settings does not differentiate between incoming and outgoing settings, 
And, in lib.py line 125 ,i believe, the exception is not casted into `e` so the actual use of this part of code would raise global name e not defined which points out that this part of code was never really executed before.
* It's also very hard to substitute small parts of the application without seriously hacking it. 
You can use options like dotted path for critical functions .. something like  `SEND_TEMPLATED_EMAIL_FUNCTION = 'myapp.handlers.mycustomfunction' `
So i can tweak this critical procedure if i want without overriding forms methods that calls inside the send function. Also you can use swappable models for model tweaking.

Template design:
And i mean the blocks.. It's very hard to simply edit, or integrate the html into the exiting  theme with blocks name always having `helpdesk_` as a prefix in them instead of the generic `extra_head`, `content` etc.

While of course this app is serving you and your use-case at its best, but it's not yet designed to be easily configured for other different uses. And the fact that you're the highest helpdesk/ticketing rated package on djangopackage should (i believe) direct development more towards making it easier to configure, not hack.

Thank you for your time reading this, and for publishing your work -of course- and again.

get_dates is tested

All radiation components provide fluxes. `GrayLongwaveRadiation` currently uses the suffix `on_interface_levels` whereas `RRTMG*` do not.

One of the reasons to use that suffix was to distinguish between the same quantity on different levels.
To my knowledge, fluxes are always defined on interface levels, making the suffix redundant. This also
makes the label really unwieldy: `upwelling_longwave_flux_in_air_on_interface_levels` or `upwelling_longwave_flux_in_air_on_interface_levels_assuming_clear_sky` is really too long,
especially when it does not seem necessary.

I propose we do not use the suffix for these quantities. 

If in the future we include a radiation component which actually outputs fluxes on model levels, we could bring it back.

issue_title: provide way to make requests to KE-chain in a parallel fashion (Thread pool)
issue_body:
We have a case that there is code that loops over the following usecase many times

1. create a part from a model under a parent
2. get the part and update its properties

we know the model_id and know the parent_id and have a list of dicts to update each part.

As pykechain is synchronously calling KEC we can speed up bulk creation of the parts dramatically by usuing requests in e.g.  threadpool. 

solution ways:
1. bulk create and update in pyke, with a threadpool of parallel requests using part.add and part.update methods in sequence
2. create the desired KEC api endpoint to bulk update a single part (detail route) or bulk create parts with their properties (list route).

As we cannot control KEC code from this repo we could provide the parallel connection solution path.

sources:
* https://github.com/kennethreitz/grequests
* http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#blocking-or-non-blocking

issue_title: Move `verify` functionality out of `index.datasets.add` into separate function
issue_body:
This is part of confusing `sources_policy=verify|ensure|skip` discussion #451.

`index.datasets.add`  doesn't just add datasets to the index (possibly including lineage datasets), it also performs consistency checks for those datasets that are already present in the database. This leads to a number of usability problems: #477, #475.

Verification is a complex task, that is likely to have many different modes. It is also a high-level task that should not be delegated to low level db driver, and currently it is implemented inside postgres db layer.

Ideally I would like `add` at the db layer to have a pre-condition that all lineage sources are already in the DB, and move recursive addition to a higher DB-independent layer. I don't see much performance gain potential for delegating lineage tree traversal and recursive add to DB layer driver. And certainly verification of "consistency" is **not** a db driver concern. Same applies for safe/unsafe update decision, which is also currently inside db-layer.

Hello Data Cube Developers - 

I've recently run into the Open Data Cube project and I'm encouraged to see that it shares many of the same underpinnings as the [Pangeo Data project](https://pangeo-data.github.io/). I'm opening this issue to make the connections between the two projects and to see if there are specific areas where the two projects may leverage each others development efforts. Some topics that may be useful to discuss if/where there is interest:

1. For every resolution, we return the log of the delta between the two inputs ([example here](https://github.com/dedupeio/datetime-distance/blob/master/datetime_distance/__init__.py#L91)). https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/6270bb68c54647f6c9ca56d640a73fe2dec2ad80/onnx/backend/test/case/node/pow.py#L49-L60

The case above seems wrong.
As [docs](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#pow) says, 
```
If necessary the right-hand-side argument will be broadcasted to match the shape of left-hand-side argument. 
When broadcasting is specified, the second tensor can either be of size 1 (a scalar value), or having its shape as a contiguous subset of the first tensor's shape. 
The starting of the mutually equal shape is specified by the argument "axis", and if it is not set, suffix matching is assumed. 1-dim expansion doesn't work yet.
```

So in this case, shape of `x` is (2, 3), `axis=0`, then shape of `y` should be in `((2,), (2,3))`.
But shape of `y = np.array([2, 3, 4]).astype(np.float32)` is (3,).

Did I miss something?
If not, I will create a pr to fix this.

issue_title: Could we have an option to exclude MemTimer's memory from the sum of children's memories
issue_body:
It occurred to me that when I create a MemTimer object with include_children=True to monitor the memory usage of *current* process, the process of MemTimer will be one of the children and its memory consumption will be included in the sum. On Linux system forking can copy majority of the memory space to the new process, leading to a spuriously large memory consumption in the recording, effectively double counting.
I would like to have an option to exclude the memory usage of the MemTimer process in such scenario.  Any idea/suggestion is appreciated. Thanks!

issue_title: Dialect.as_python_formatting_parameters() escapechar not in line with CSVW spec
issue_body:
Currently, we set Python `escapechar` to `\\` whenever CSVW `quoteChar` is set:

https://github.com/cldf/csvw/blob/f0869818eb8c34949809f1b24a7e1d30cd0adb51/csvw/dsv_dialects.py#L112

However, the [spec](https://w3c.github.io/csvw/syntax/#x8-parsing-tabular-data) says:

> escape character
> The string that is used to escape the quote character within escaped cells, or null,
> set by the doubleQuote property of a dialect description. The default is " (such that ""
> is used to escape " within an escaped cell).

Python `csv` is in line with this (apart from what variable controls what) :

> Dialect.escapechar
> A one-character string used by the writer to escape the delimiter if quoting is set to QUOTE_NONE
> and the quotechar if doublequote is False.

IIUC, `QUOTE_NONE` is not allowed by CSVW at all and Python `escapechar` should **not** be set with `doublequote=True` (i.e. Python `doublequote=True, escapechar=None` corresponds to CSVW `doubleQuote=True`, which sets `escape character` to `"`).

In other words, the line above would need to be changed to:

```python
'escapechar': self.escape_character if not self.doubleQuote and self.quoteChar not in ('"', None) else None,
```

In this case the following test cases would be ill-formed:

https://github.com/cldf/csvw/blob/f0869818eb8c34949809f1b24a7e1d30cd0adb51/tests/test_metadata.py#L57

https://github.com/cldf/csvw/blob/f0869818eb8c34949809f1b24a7e1d30cd0adb51/tests/test_dsv.py#L147

Side note: AFAIU, neither Python nor CSVW escape the **delimiter**, so even with `doubleQuote=False`, the writer would not produce `y\\,x` (escaped delimiter) but rather `"y,x"` (quoted field) for cells containing the delimiter, although the former is parsed correctly by Python `csv`:

```python
In [1]: import io, csv
    ...: f = io.StringIO('spam\\,eggs,spam', newline='')
    ...: reader = csv.reader(f, doublequote=False, escapechar='\\')
    ...: next(reader)
Out[1]: ['spam,eggs', 'spam']
```
